width="90%" fig-alt="Google Colab logo."}

**How Colab compares to local Jupyter:**

| Feature | Google Colab | Local Jupyter/JupyterLab |
|----|----|----|
| **Start time** | Instant (browser) | \~10 seconds (conda activate + jupyter lab) |
| **Free GPU** | ✅ Yes (T4/V100) | ❌ Depends on your hardware |
| **Works offline** | ❌ Requires internet | ✅ Fully offline |
| **Persistent files** | Via Google Drive only | Local disk (always available) |
| **Sensitive data** | ⚠️ Not recommended | ✅ Stays on your machine |
| **Package install** | `!pip install` per session | `conda install` once per env |

> **How to get started:** Go to [colab.research.google.com](https://colab.research.google.com), sign in with a Google account, and click **New notebook**. Your notebook is saved automatically to Google Drive.
:::

## 🖥️ Terminal / REPL

For production work, automation, and server-side analysis:

```{bash terminal_usage}
# Run a Python script directly
python analysis.py

# Run with arguments
python analysis.py --input data.csv --output results.csv

# Interactive Python REPL
python

# IPython — enhanced REPL (included with Anaconda)
ipython

# Run a one-liner
python -c "import scipy; print(scipy.__version__)"
```

| Use Jupyter/JupyterLab      | Use the terminal                        |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Exploratory data analysis   | Running finished analysis automatically |
| Teaching or presenting      | Scheduled (cron) jobs                   |
| Sharing interactive reports | Processing data on a server             |
| Learning Python             | CI/CD pipelines                         |
:::::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 🚀 Complete Workflow: Launch → Analyse → Visualise

Follow these steps to run your first real statistical analysis in Python.
:::

### Step 1 — Launch your environment

```{bash first_session_launch}
conda activate stats_project    # Activate your environment
cd ~/Documents/stats_project    # Navigate to your project
jupyter lab                     # Open JupyterLab
```

### Step 2 — Set up your notebook

```{python first_cell_imports}
# Cell 1: Import all libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import scipy.stats as stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="deep")
%matplotlib inline
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

print("✅ Libraries loaded successfully!")
print(f"   numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"   pandas  {pd.__version__}")
```

### Step 3 — Create data and run a t-test

```{python first_cell_ttest}
# Cell 2: Simulate two groups and run an independent t-test
np.random.seed(2024)
control   = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=30)
treatment = np.random.normal(loc=56, scale=10, size=30)

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment)
print(f"t-statistic = {t_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value     = {p_value:.4f}")
print("→ Statistically significant!" if p_value < 0.05 else "→ Not significant at α = 0.05")
```

### Step 4 — Visualise

```{python first_cell_plot}
# Cell 3: Boxplot + strip plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

# Left: Boxplot
axes[0].boxplot([control, treatment], labels=["Control", "Treatment"],
                patch_artist=True,
                boxprops=dict(facecolor="#dbeafe", color="#1d4ed8"),
                medianprops=dict(color="#dc2626", linewidth=2))
axes[0].set_ylabel("Score")
axes[0].set_title(f"Independent t-test\nt = {t_stat:.2f},  p = {p_value:.4f}")

# Right: Distributions
x = np.linspace(20, 90, 300)
for grp, col, lbl in [(control,"#3b82f6","Control"),(treatment,"#ef4444","Treatment")]:
    mu, sd = grp.mean(), grp.std(ddof=1)
    axes[1].plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sd), linewidth=2.5, label=f"{lbl} (μ={mu:.1f})", color=col)
    axes[1].hist(grp, bins=12, density=True, alpha=0.25, color=col)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Score")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Density")
axes[1].set_title("Overlapping Distributions")
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle("First Statistical Analysis in Python", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Summary and Next Steps

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## ✅ Modules 1–3 at a Glance

```{r summary_final, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
df_sum <- data.frame(
  Module = c("1 · What is Python?","2 · Installation & Setup","3 · Python Environment"),
  `Core Message` = c(
    "Python 3 is free, readable, interpreted. Complements R; replaces SPSS/MATLAB for most tasks.",
    "Use Anaconda. conda manages packages & envs. One environment per project. Verify with conda --version.",
    "Jupyter/JupyterLab for analysis. VSCode for scripting. Colab for quick cloud work. Restart kernel before sharing."
  ),
  `Statistical Value` = c(
    "Superior to SPSS for reproducibility; equal to R for stats; better than R for ML pipelines.",
    "scipy, statsmodels, pandas install in one conda command. No manual library management.",
    "Notebook format = statistical report + code + output in one file. Ideal for reproducible research."
  ),
  check.names = FALSE
)
knitr::kable(df_sum, caption="Modules 1–3 Summary")
```
:::

### 📚 References

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 📖 Recommended Reading

1.  **VanderPlas, J.** (2016). *Python Data Science Handbook: Essential Tools for Working with Data*. O'Reilly.\
    🌐 Free online: <https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook/>

2.  **Rogel-Salazar, J.** (2017). *Data Science and Analytics with Python*. CRC Press / Chapman and Hall.

3.  **Python Software Foundation.** <https://www.python.org>

4.  **Anaconda Inc.** <https://www.anaconda.com/download>

5.  **Project Jupyter.** <https://jupyter.org>
:::

### 🔜 Coming Up Next

| Module | Topics |
|----|----|
| **Python Basics** | Variables, data types, operators, control flow (`if`/`for`/`while`), functions, data structures (list/dict/tuple/set), modules & imports |
| **Key Libraries** | NumPy arrays & maths · pandas DataFrames · matplotlib/seaborn visualisation · SciPy statistical tests · statsmodels regression |
| **Capstone Project** | Load a real CSV → descriptive stats → hypothesis test → visualisation → Quarto report |
